revision.comment: <ref>タグの重複、リンク切れ修復（Wayback）
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 諏訪神社 |画像 = [[File:Suwa Shrine 20190513(2).jpg|250px|]]
諏訪神社本殿 |所在地 = 立川市柴崎町1丁目5番15号{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=4}} |位置={{Coord|35|41|43.4|N|139|24|22.0|E|region:JP-13_type:landmark|display=it}} |祭神 = 建御名方神{{sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}} |神体 = |社格 = 郷社{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=6}} |創建 = 811年（弘仁2年）7月27日{{efn|『[[江戸名所図会]]』では7月21日{{sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}}。}} |本殿 = |別名 =おすわさま{{Sfn|東京都神社庁|1986|p=322}} |札所等 = |例祭 = 8月第4土・日曜日 |神事 = |地図 = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|frame-height=150|frame-latitude=35.707|frame-longitude=139.405}}{{Location map|Japan Tokyo|float=center|width=210}} }} '''諏訪神社'''（すわじんじゃ）は[[東京都]][[立川市]][[柴崎町]]にある神社。地名を冠して'''立川諏訪神社'''（たちかわすわじんじゃ）とも称される。[[獅子舞]]と[[相撲]]の名社とされている{{Cite web|和書|title=諏訪神社【おすわさま】|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/tachikawa/3063/|publisher=東京都神社庁|accessdate=2019-05-13}}。 この項では本殿に並んで祀られている合祀社の八幡神社、末社の稲荷神社についても記す。 == 歴史 == === 創建 === 諏訪神社に伝わる由緒書によれば、[[811年]]（[[弘仁]]2年）7月27日信濃国から諏訪大神を、柴崎村出口（現立川市柴崎町、諏訪の森公園）に勧進鎭祭したのが創建とされる{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=立川の神社建築調査報告 |publisher=立川市教育委員会 |year=1989 |pages=106-107 |isbn=}}{{Cite web|和書|title=由緒|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/history.html|publisher=諏訪神社|accessdate=2019-05-13}}。 諏訪の森公園に建つ「旧宮址」の石碑には、「弘仁弐年六月弐十七日　勧請信州諏訪大神」と刻まれている。 天正14年武蔵野野火 - [[1586年]]（[[天正]]14年）11月9日、武蔵野に野火が起こり、社殿・宝物・旧記・神主の系図などが消失。 === 江戸時代以降の歴史 === 武蔵野野火での消失以降は以下の通り。 * 1670年（[[寛文]]10年）、従来の場所から社地を移して、諏訪神社本殿再建。 * 1741年（[[寛保]]元年）：本殿造立。 * 1750年（[[寛延]]3年）：本殿造立。 * 1787年（[[天明]]7年）：日吉神社神殿造立。 * 1804年（[[文化 (元号)|文化]]元年）：本殿破壊・修復。 * 1819年（[[文政]]2年）：境内末社稲荷神社創建。 * 1824年（文政7年）：八幡神社火災で焼失。 * 1841年（[[天保]]12年）：八幡神社再建。 * 1853年（[[嘉永]]6年）：金毘羅神社創建。 * 1907年（[[明治]]40年）：諏訪神社本殿に八幡神社ご神体を合祀。 * 1907年（明治40年）：境内末社に津島神社を祀る。 * 1910年（明治43年）：浅間神社合祀。 * 1914年（[[大正]]3年）：稲荷神社・日吉神社・金毘羅神社改築。 * 1930年（[[昭和]]5年）：大鷲神社を津島神社に勧進・合祀。 * 1954年（昭和26年）：諏訪神社、宗教法人となる。 * 1964年（昭和39年）：八幡神社本殿、覆舎改築。 * 1994年（[[平成]]6年）10月26日：神社焼失。 * 2002年（平成14年）4月7日：諏訪神社・八幡神社・稲荷神社本殿が再建。 * 2021年（[[令和]]3年）6月：立川ロータリークラブが、[[新型コロナウイルス感染症の世界的流行 (2019年-)|新型コロナウィルス感染拡大]]下で、疫病から人々を守るとされる[[アマビエ]]像を寄贈。 == 祭神 == [[建御名方神]]（たけみかたのかみ） この神は[[大国主神]]（おおくにぬしのかみ）の2番目の子。 昔から軍神と農耕の神として祭られる{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}}。 * 浅間神社：[[木花之佐久夜毘売命]]（このはなさくやひめのみこと） * 日吉神社：大山咋神（おおやまくいのかみ） * 金刀比羅神社：崇徳天皇{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}}、大物主命（おおものぬしのみこと）{{Sfn|東京都神社庁|1986}} * 疱瘡神社：少彦名命（すくなひこなのみこと） * 目の神社 == 境内 == [[File:立川諏訪神社の見取り図.png|400px|right|thumb|立川諏訪神社の見取り図]] === 神楽殿 === [[1929年]]（昭和4年）神楽殿新築工事完成{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}}。[[1966年]]（昭和41年）台風により倒壊{{Sfn|諏訪神社社務所|2002|p=39-41}}。[[1968年]]（昭和43年）新神楽殿完成{{Sfn|諏訪神社社務所|2002|p=39-41}}。 === 社務所 === [[1909年]]（明治42年）初めて社務所を設置{{Sfn|諏訪神社社務所|2002|p=39-41}}。 === 境内末社 === * 疱瘡神社：創建年月は不詳{{Sfn|諏訪神社社務所|2002|p=3}}。 * 日吉神社：[[811年]]（[[弘仁]]2年）7月27日創建{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}}。御祭神は大山咋神（おおやまくいのかみ）[[1787年]]（[[天明]]7年）神殿造立{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}}。[[1914年]]（大正3年)に改築{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}}。 * 金毘羅神社：[[1853年]]（[[嘉永]]6年）創建{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}}。御祭神は崇徳天皇{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}}。[[1914年]]（大正3年）に改築{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}}。 * 浅間神社：以前は、上古新田の富士塚（現：立川市富士見町1丁目）にあった。御祭神は木花之佐久夜昆売命（このはなさくやひめのみこと）{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}}。 創建年代は不明。江戸時代には天台宗延暦寺派妙覚寺に付属していた{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}}。維新で、祭祀の礼が絶えたことから、[[1910年]]（明治43年）に諏訪神社に合祀{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}}。 * 目の神様：創建年月は不詳{{Sfn|諏訪神社社務所|2002|p=3}}である。以前この地にもみの大木が立っており、その幹を、住民が崇めるようになり、生み出された神様だと言い伝えられている。{{Cite book|和書|author=立川市商店街振興組合連合会 |title=立川まち探訪ガイド |publisher=立川市・立川市商店街振興組合 |year=2011 |page=27 |isbn=}} === その他の施設など === * 殉国慰霊碑：[[1963年]]（昭和38年）建立{{Sfn|諏訪神社社務所|2002|p=39-41}}。 * 諏訪神社御鎮座壱千弐百年の記 * 随身像 * 土俵 - 例祭にて奉納相撲が行われる{{Cite magazine |和書 |date=2018-07-25 |title=特集 立川の祭り |magazine=公報たちかわ |issue=1468 |publisher=立川市 |format=PDF |url=https://www.city.tachikawa.lg.jp/koho/shise/koho/kohotachikawa/h30/documents/20180725s.pdf |accessdate=2019-05-13}}。 * 立川市練成館 - 柔道・剣道・弓道・相撲の練習場{{Cite web|和書|title=練成館|url=http://www.city.tachikawa.lg.jp/supotsushinko/shisetsu/sport/001.html|website=立川市|accessdate=2019-05-13|last=立川市}} * 参集殿 == 文化財 == * 諏訪神社獅子頭及び太鼓胴：1963年（昭和38年）6月22日立川市の文化財に指定。雄獅子1、雌獅子1、太鼓胴3が残っており、[[元禄]]ごろに制作されたもの。 柴崎町、富士見町などで伝承されてきた獅子舞で使用されていた。 *諏訪神社の獅子舞：立川市の無形民俗文化財。演目は、一通り、二舞い込み、三おかざき、四向いで、五鹿島、六雌獅子がかり、七松山、八腰やすめ、九とびきり、十十七、十一山がら、十二さらさら、がある。「十二狂い」と言われ、「十二にわ」舞うという。演目それぞれに唄が伴われ、棒使いが「ヤアットォ」と声をかける。獅子を舞う人数は、牡獅子二人、牝獅子一人、天狗一人。囃し方として、法螺貝一人、笛数人、唄方数人で行われる{{Sfn|立川市史編纂委員会|1978|p=1290-1293}}。獅子舞の先払い役として棒使いがあり、山中（富士見町）の五十嵐家が、[[三河国]]で棒術を習ったと伝えられる。鈴木平九郎が天保年間から安政年間に記した「公私日記」には、獅子舞と棒使いがそれぞれ宿で稽古や支度をして、祭礼に臨んだと記されている。宿は、地区内の家が当番で務め、食事や風呂の接待を行った。負担が大きいが名誉なこととされていた。農家の減少とともに、宿の確保が難しくなり、公民館のような共同施設が使われるようになった。1963年（[[昭和]]38年）に、獅子舞の保存と継承のために「諏訪神社獅子舞保存会」が設立された{{Cite journal|和書|journal=多摩のあゆみ |title=ベッドタウンで「伝統」と向き合う―立川市諏訪神社の獅子舞のあゆみー |volume= |issue=180}}。現在、獅子舞は、「立川市獅子舞芸能保存会」によって、保存・継承されている。同会には、令和元年現在、小学生から80歳代までの30人ほどが所属。獅子舞は、高校生や大学生が演ずる。棒使いは、小学生が演じる。獅子舞に興味を持ち、継承する若手を増やすために、地元の小中学校で獅子舞の授業を行っている{{Wayback|url=https://www.city.tachikawa.lg.jp/shogaigakushu/kosodate/kyoiku/iinkai/shogaigakushu/kirari/46-50.html |title=たちかわ市民交流大学情報誌きらり・たちかわ第48号 |date=20200923164547}}。 == 八幡神社 == {{神社 |名称 = 八幡神社 |画像 =[[File:Suwa Shrine 20190513(3).jpg|200px|]] |所在地 = 東京都立川市柴崎町1-5-15 諏訪神社内 | 緯度度 = |緯度分 = |緯度秒 = | 経度度 = |経度分 = |経度秒 = |祭神 = 誉田別命（ホムタワケノミコト）{{sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=392}} |神体 = |社格 = |創建 = 1252年（[[建長]]4年）8月15日{{sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=392}} |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = |神事 = |地図 = }} 諏訪神社本殿は、正面に諏訪神社、左手に八幡神社、右手に稲荷神社が配されており、建造物として他の末社とは分けられている。 八幡神社（はちまんじんじゃ）は1907年（明治40年）7月まで東京都立川市柴崎町1-7-17にあり、以降は諏訪神社に合祀されている神社。 1252年（[[建長]]4年）8月15日に創建され、当時現在の立川市にて勢力をふるっていた[[立川氏]]の氏神として代々擁護を受けてきた{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=4-5}}。 1586年（[[天正]]14年）の野火で諏訪神社とともに社殿が焼失し、諏訪神社に先立って再建されたが、さらに1824年（[[文政]]7年）に再び火災に見舞われた。1841年（[[天保]]12年）に改めて造営された。 明治の中頃、村内で秋蚕の生産が盛んになったため、従来8月15日に行われていた祭礼が9月15日に変更された{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=諏訪神社平成再建社殿　再建の記録 |publisher=諏訪神社　社務所 |year=2002-04-07 |page=40 |isbn=}}。 しかし、1907年（明治40年）11月、無住のため社殿の維持が困難になったため、御神体を諏訪神社に合祀、旧本殿も併せて神社境内に移築された。 1994年（平成6年）10月26日の不審火で焼失し、諏訪神社とともに再建された{{Sfn|立川市の獅子舞のあゆみ編集委員会|2004|p=29}}。 諏訪神社内で保管されている八幡神社本地仏像は[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]の立川氏を知る重要な資料として知られている{{Cite magazine |和書 |date=2015-11-15 |magazine=多摩のあゆみ |issue=160 |page=25 |publisher=公益財団法人 たましん地域文化財団 |issn=09139680}}。 === 八幡神社大欅 === 立川市柴崎町1丁目7番7号先にある、立川市内最大級の樹木で、立川市指定天然記念物（1955年〈昭和30年〉7月11日）{{Cite web|和書|title=指定文化財等一覧|url=http://www.city.tachikawa.lg.jp/shogaigakushu/kosodate/kyoiku/iinkai/shiryokan/bunkazai/bunkazai.html|website=立川市教育委員会|accessdate=2019-05-13|last=立川市教育委員会}}。 八幡神社創建当時に植えられたとされている{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=立川の老樹・名木 |publisher=立川市教育委員会 |year=1986 |pages=9-10 |isbn=}}。指定当時、周囲6メートル、高さ28メートルあった。2000年の観測では樹齢が300年以上、周囲6メートル、高さが9メートルとなっている{{Cite web|和書|url=https://kyoju.biodic.go.jp/|title=巨樹・巨木材データベース|accessdate=2019-05-14|publisher=環境省}}。 === 柴一八幡公会堂 === 別名「獅子宿」と呼ばれている立川市にある公会堂。八幡神社大欅の向かい側に位置し、毎年8月に催される諏訪八幡両神社例大祭に奉納する獅子舞（1967年〈昭和42年〉9月13日立川市無形民俗文化財指定）の練習場に使用されている。この獅子舞は元禄時代からの伝承と伝えられており、祭礼当日にはこの公会堂から諏訪八幡両神社に隊列を組んで出発し、神社境内において獅子舞を演舞奉納している{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=立川市自治会連合会 創立50周年記念誌 |publisher=立川市自治会連合会 |year=2016 |pages=93-94 |isbn=}}。 === 八幡神社本殿跡 === 柴崎町1-7-17にある。1955年（昭和30年）3月10日立川市の文化財に指定。1252年（建長4年）8月15日に鎮祭され、その後2度（天正14年、文政7年）の火災により、1841年（天保12年）に再造営された。 その後、1907年（明治40年）11月27日に当時の諏訪神社拝殿東側に移築された。 == 稲荷神社 == {{神社 |名称 = 稲荷神社 |画像 =[[File:Suwa Shrine 20190515(1).jpg|200px|]] |所在地 = 立川市柴崎町1丁目5番15号（諏訪神社内） | 緯度度 = |緯度分 = |緯度秒 = | 経度度 = |経度分 = |経度秒 = |祭神 = 宇迦御魂神（ウカノミタマノカミ） |神体 = |社格 = |創建 = 1819年（文政2年）6月 |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = |神事 = |地図 = }} === 祭神 === [[ウカノミタマ|宇迦御魂神]]（うかのみたまのかみ） === 歴史 === 1819年（文政2年）6月の創建。1914年（大正3年）5月に旧社殿の形に造営される。1994年（平成6年）10月26日の火災により、焼失。2001年（平成13年）7月末日に本殿と共に現在の形となる{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}}{{Sfn|立川市の獅子舞のあゆみ編集委員会|2004|p=30}}。 == 年中行事 == 年中行事は以下のとおりである{{Cite web|和書|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html |title=年中行事 |publisher =諏訪神社|accessdate =2019-05-13}}。 * 1月1日 - 歳旦祭 * 1月3日 - 新年交通安全祈願祭 * 1月15日 - 成人祭・[[左義長|ドンドン焼き]] * 2月3日 - 節分祭（豆まき） * 午の日 - 初午祭・二の午祭（稲荷神社） * 2月11日 - 建国祭 * 4月10日 - 末社金刀毘羅神社祭 * 4月17日 - 春祭り（祈年祭） * 5月上申日 - 末社日吉神社祭 * 7月25日 - 夏祭り（天王祭） * 8月28日直近の金曜 - 例大祭宵宮祭 土曜 - 例大祭（諏訪神社） 日曜 - 例大祭（八幡神社） ** 例大祭は戦前から、奉納相撲と獅子舞は300年以上の歴史がある{{Cite web|和書|title=立川市で夏祭りシーズン始まる－市内各所で毎週末踊りやパレード|url=https://tachikawa.keizai.biz/headline/1259/|website=立川経済新聞|accessdate=2019-05-14}}。境内には露店が250軒以上も軒を連ねる年もある{{Cite web|和書|title=諏訪神社例大祭 {{!}} アッタカナ立川|url=http://tachikawa.attakana.com/archives/2087|website=tachikawa.attakana.com|accessdate=2019-05-14}}。 **地元の自治会や商店街主催の祭り「立川諏訪祭り」が、例大祭に合わせて開催され、神輿や太鼓、山車、お囃子などが出て、祭りに活気を添えている。また、神輿の「宮出し」「宮入り」では、神輿がやっと通り抜けられる大きさの隋身門（神門）を神輿が通り抜ける様子が、祭りの見どころとなっている。 * 9月15日 - 秋祭り・奉納芸能大会 * 11月1日 - 菊花展（15日まで） * 酉の日 - 一の酉・二の酉・三の酉（大鷲神社） * 11月15日 - 七五三祭 * 11月27日 - 新嘗祭 * 12月31日 - 除夜祭 * 毎月1日 - 月次祭 == その他 == [[中村光 (漫画家)|中村光]]著の漫画『[[聖☆おにいさん]]』の舞台は東京都立川市とされており、作中に諏訪神社が登場する{{Cite web |url=http://www.buccyake-kojiki.com/archives/1023737578.html |title=諏訪神社　東京都立川市柴崎町 |work=神社と古事記 |accessdate =2019-05-13}}。 2013年（平成25年）7月3日に行われた立川駅北口西地区の再開発に伴う起工式では、諏訪神社の神職によって祝詞が奏上された{{Cite web|和書|title=立川駅北口西地区の再開発で起工式－2016年完成目指す|url=https://tachikawa.keizai.biz/headline/1483/|website=立川経済新聞|accessdate=2019-05-14}}。2016年（平成28年）に竣工し名称は[[立川タクロス]]となった{{Cite web|和書|title=立川駅北口西地区第一種市街地再開発事業|url=http://www.city.tachikawa.lg.jp/saikaihatsu/shise/toshizukuri/toshi/chushin/ekikitaguchinishichikukaihatsu.html|website=立川市|accessdate=2019-05-14|language=ja|last=立川市}}。 テレビドラマ「ごくせん」のロケ地として使用された{{Cite web|和書|url=http://loca.ash.jp/show/2008/d200804_gokusen.htm |title=全国ロケ地ガイド |publisher =|accessdate =2019-06-10}}。 === 火災と再建 === 1994年（平成6年）10月26日未明、火災が発生し、諏訪神社・八幡神社の建物、事務所、結婚式場を延焼した{{Cite book|和書|author=朝日新聞 |title=諏訪神社の本殿など全焼　不審火の疑い　立川 |year=1994.10.26 |page=夕刊１５面 }}。発生時間の午前2時ごろは、神社関係者は就寝中だったため、火災報知器の音で気付くも消火ができない状態だった。 指定文化財のうち、諏訪神社本殿付棟札、旧八幡神社本殿付棟札、諏訪神社獅子頭及び太鼓胴は、宝物殿に保管されていて無事であった。八幡神社本地仏像は、立川市歴史民俗資料館に保管されていて無事であった。 諏訪神社は、消火作業で引き倒されていたが、梁の組み構造は残っていた。八幡神社は、建物本体の骨組や、彫刻が残っていた。 諏訪神社は、被災後すぐに再建の方針を決め、再建委員会を設置した。 立川市が、指定文化財の被害の調査を行ったところ、本殿についての建築調査を（昭和60年）（昭和61年）に行い、報告書{{Cite book|和書|author=立川市教育委員会 |title=立川の神社建築調査報告（立川市文化財調査報告６） |publisher=立川市教育委員会 |year=1989 |page= |isbn=}}を作成していたため、復元の可能性があることがわかった。本殿の焼け跡の部材の解体調査を行い、データを取得した{{Cite book|和書|author=立川市教育委員会 |title=立川の文化財1994 |publisher=立川市教育委員会 |year=1995 |page=3-7 |isbn=}}。 この結果をもとに、株式会社臼井裕泰空間文化設計所の設計、清水建設株式会社の施工により、諏訪神社を再建した。再建にあたっては、調査によって明らかになった結果をもとに、文化財として復原した。 さらに、諏訪神社本殿を本神、八幡神社本殿を祖先神、稲荷神社本殿を土地神と想定し、一つの覆舎の中に、三神並列の形式で祭祀した{{Cite book|和書|author=諏訪神社社務所 |title=諏訪神社平成再建社殿再建の記録 |publisher=諏訪神社社務所 |year=2002 |page=6-9 |isbn=}}。 === 諏訪通り === 諏訪神社への参道である諏訪通りは、現在諏訪通り商店街となっている。 「諏訪通り」の名称がいつから使用されているのかは、明らかではない。 以前は、さまざまな名称で呼ばれていたようである。 明治末期生まれの住民の記憶に基づいて作成された地図「大正6年頃の原市場通り（現在の諏訪通り）の人家」が、歴史民俗博物館に所蔵されており、「立川の昭和史　第二集　昭和初期の耕地整理と鉄道網の発達」にも掲載されている。また、この住民の記憶によれば、「諏訪通り」は、以前は「役場通り」とも呼ばれていた。大正2年までは、立川村役場が、諏訪神社の南にあったためではないかと考えられる。さらに、この住民の記憶によれば、原市場は、諏訪神社の諏訪通りをはさんだ向かい側にあった。 また、昭和8年12月の立川印刷所のチラシには、諏訪通りを「小学校通り」と記している。現在の[[立川市立第一小学校|第一小学校]]は、大正３年に諏訪神社に隣接する位置に移転したことから、このように呼ばれたようである。{{Cite book|和書|author=立川市教育委員会 |title=立川の昭和史　第二集　昭和初期の耕地整理と鉄道網の発達 |publisher=立川市教育委員会 |year=1999 |page=163-171 |isbn=}}。 == ギャラリー == ファイル:Suwa Shrine 20190825-1.jpg|旧宮址　 ファイル:Suwa Shrine 20190515(10).jpg|神門 ファイル:Suwa Shrine 20190515(4-2).png|神楽殿 ファイル:Suwa Shrine 20190515(5).jpg|境内末社 ファイル:Suwa Shrine 20190515(6).jpg|目の神様 ファイル:Suwa Shrine 20190515(7).jpg|八幡神社大欅 ファイル:Suwa Shrine 20190515(9).jpg|八幡神社本殿跡 ファイル:Suwa Shrine 20190826-1.jpg|例大祭での神輿 ファイル:アマビエ像.jpg|[[アマビエ]]像 ==交通アクセス== 最寄り駅：[[東日本旅客鉄道|JR東日本]] [[立川駅]]から徒歩10分{{Cite book |和書 |author=スポーツ祭東京2013実行委員会 |title=東京39ふるさとものがたり |date=2013-09-28}} ==脚注== {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{notelist}} === 出典 === {{Reflist|2}} == 参考文献 == * 2002 |edition = |publisher = 諏訪神社社務所 |isbn = |series = |ref = harv}} * {{Cite book |和書 |author=立川市の獅子舞のあゆみ編集委員会 |title=立川市の獅子舞のあゆみ |year=2004 |publisher=立川市獅子舞芸能保存会 |isbn= |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book |和書 |author=東京都神社庁 |title=東京都神社名鑑 |volume=下巻 |date=1986-03-30 |publisher=東京都神社庁 |ncid=BN04039320 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book |和書 |author=立川市史編纂委員会 |title=立川市史 |volume=下巻 |date=1978 |publisher=立川市 |ref=harv}} * {{cite book|和書|title=[[江戸名所図会]]|id={{NDLJP|1174144/200}}|publisher=有朋堂書店|editor=斎藤長秋|volume=2|series=有朋堂文庫|year=1927|chapter=巻之三 天璣之部 立川 八幡宮 諏訪社|pages=390-394|ref={{sfnref|江戸名所図会|1927}}}} == ゆかりのある人物 == * [[真如苑]] == 外部リンク == {{CommonsCat}} * {{Official|suwajinja.or.jp}} {{DEFAULTSORT:すわしんしや たちかわししはさきちよう}} [[Category:東京都の神社]] revision.text:
{{Shrine |Name = Suwa Shrine |Image = [[File:Suwa Shrine 20190513(2).jpg|250px|]]
Suwa Shrine Main Hall |Location = 1-5-15 Shibasaki-cho, Tachikawa City, Tokyo{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=4}} |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|43.4|N|139|24|22.0|E|region:JP-13_type:landmark|display=it}} |Deity = {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方神}}{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}} |SacredObject = |Rank = {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=6}} |Founded = July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2){{efn|The [[江戸名所図会]] gives the date as July 21{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}}.}} |MainHall = |Alias = Osuwasama{{Sfn|東京都神社庁|1986|p=322}} |Festival = Fourth Saturday and Sunday in August |Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|frame-height=150|frame-latitude=35.707|frame-longitude=139.405}}{{Location map|Japan Tokyo|float=center|width=210}} }} '''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tachikawa City]], [[Tokyo]], Japan. Also referred to as '''Tachikawa Suwa Shrine''' (立川諏訪神社), it is renowned for its [[shishi-mai|lion dances]] and [[sumo]] performances.{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine 【Osuwasama】|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/tachikawa/3063/|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Association|accessdate=2019-05-13}} This article also covers the Hachiman Shrine, which is enshrined alongside Suwa Shrine’s main hall, and the subsidiary Inari Shrine. == History == ### Founding According to shrine records, Suwa Shrine was established on July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2), when the deity {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方命}} from {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} was enshrined in Shibasaki Village (modern-day Suwanomori Park, Tachikawa City).{{Cite book|title=Report on Tachikawa’s Shrine Architecture Survey|publisher=Tachikawa City Board of Education|year=1989|pages=106-107}}{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/history.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} The stone monument at Suwanomori Park bears the inscription, "June 27, Kōnin 2 – Enshrined Shinano Suwa Great Deity." ### Fire of 1586 A wildfire occurred on November 9, 1586 (Tenshō 14), destroying the shrine's main hall, treasures, historical records, and the genealogy of the priests. ### Edo Period to Present Subsequent key events include: * 1670 (Kanbun 10): Relocation of the shrine and reconstruction of the main hall. * 1741 (Kanpō 1) & 1750 (Kan’en 3): Main hall rebuilt. * 1819 (Bunsei 2): Founding of the subsidiary Inari Shrine. * 1841 (Tenpō 12): Rebuilding of Hachiman Shrine after a fire in 1824 (Bunsei 7). * 1907 (Meiji 40): Hachiman Shrine deity incorporated into Suwa Shrine’s main hall. * 1994 (Heisei 6): Fire destroyed the shrine complex. * 2002 (Heisei 14): Reconstruction of Suwa, Hachiman, and Inari Shrines. == Deities == The primary deity of Suwa Shrine is {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方神}}, the second son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神}}. He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命}} (Asama Shrine) * {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神}} (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}} and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from [[JR East]]’s {{ill|Tachikawa Station|ja|立川駅}}.{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine, Tachikawa}} [[Category:Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Suwa Shrines]] [[Category:Tachikawa, Tokyo]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市)]]

Also referred to as '''Tachikawa Suwa Shrine''' (立川諏訪神社), it is renowned for its [[shishi-mai|lion dances]] and [[sumo]] performances.{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine 【Osuwasama】|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/tachikawa/3063/|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Association|accessdate=2019-05-13}} This article also covers the Hachiman Shrine, which is enshrined alongside Suwa Shrine’s main hall, and the subsidiary Inari Shrine. == History == ### Founding According to shrine records, Suwa Shrine was established on July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2), when the deity {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方命}} from {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} was enshrined in Shibasaki Village (modern-day Suwanomori Park, Tachikawa City).{{Cite book|title=Report on Tachikawa’s Shrine Architecture Survey|publisher=Tachikawa City Board of Education|year=1989|pages=106-107}}{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/history.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} The stone monument at Suwanomori Park bears the inscription, "June 27, Kōnin 2 – Enshrined Shinano Suwa Great deity." ### Fire of 1586 A wildfire occurred on November 9, 1586 (Tenshō 14), destroying the shrine's main hall, treasures, historical records, and the genealogy of the priests. ### Edo Period to Present Subsequent key events include: * 1670 (Kanbun 10): Relocation of the shrine and reconstruction of the main hall. * 1741 (Kanpō 1) & 1750 (Kan’en 3): Main hall rebuilt. * 1819 (Bunsei 2): Founding of the subsidiary Inari Shrine. * 1841 (Tenpō 12): Rebuilding of Hachiman Shrine after a fire in 1824 (Bunsei 7). * 1907 (Meiji 40): Hachiman Shrine deity incorporated into Suwa Shrine’s main hall. * 1994 (Heisei 6): Fire destroyed the shrine complex. * 2002 (Heisei 14): Reconstruction of Suwa, Hachiman, and Inari Shrines. == Deities == The primary deity of Suwa Shrine is {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方神}}, the second son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神}}. He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命}} (Asama Shrine) * {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神}} (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}} and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from [[JR East]]’s {{ill|Tachikawa Station|ja|立川駅}}.{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine, Tachikawa}} [[Category:Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Suwa Shrines]] [[Category:Tachikawa, Tokyo]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市)]]

Suwa Shrine Main Hall |location = 1-5-15 Shibasaki-cho, Tachikawa City, Tokyo{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=4}} |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|43.4|N|139|24|22.0|E|region:JP-13_type:landmark|display=it}} |deity = {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方神}}{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}} |SacredObject = |Rank = {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=6}} |Founded = July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2){{efn|The [[江戸名所図会]] gives the date as July 21{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}}.}} |MainHall = |Alias = Osuwasama{{Sfn|東京都神社庁|1986|p=322}} |Festival = Fourth Saturday and Sunday in August |Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|frame-height=150|frame-latitude=35.707|frame-longitude=139.405}}{{location map|Japan Tokyo|float=center|width=210}} }} '''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tachikawa City]], [[Tokyo]], Japan. Also referred to as '''Tachikawa Suwa Shrine''' (立川諏訪神社), it is renowned for its [[shishi-mai|lion dances]] and [[sumo]] performances.{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine 【Osuwasama】|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/tachikawa/3063/|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Association|accessdate=2019-05-13}} This article also covers the Hachiman Shrine, which is enshrined alongside Suwa Shrine’s main hall, and the subsidiary Inari Shrine. == History == ### Founding According to shrine records, Suwa Shrine was established on July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2), when the deity {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方命}} from {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} was enshrined in Shibasaki Village (modern-day Suwanomori Park, Tachikawa City).{{Cite book|title=Report on Tachikawa’s Shrine Architecture Survey|publisher=Tachikawa City Board of Education|year=1989|pages=106-107}}{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/history.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} The stone monument at Suwanomori Park bears the inscription, "June 27, Kōnin 2 – Enshrined Shinano Suwa Great deity." ### Fire of 1586 A wildfire occurred on November 9, 1586 (Tenshō 14), destroying the shrine's main hall, treasures, historical records, and the genealogy of the priests. ### Edo Period to Present Subsequent key events include: * 1670 (Kanbun 10): Relocation of the shrine and reconstruction of the main hall. * 1741 (Kanpō 1) & 1750 (Kan’en 3): Main hall rebuilt. * 1819 (Bunsei 2): Founding of the subsidiary Inari Shrine. * 1841 (Tenpō 12): Rebuilding of Hachiman Shrine after a fire in 1824 (Bunsei 7). * 1907 (Meiji 40): Hachiman Shrine deity incorporated into Suwa Shrine’s main hall. * 1994 (Heisei 6): Fire destroyed the shrine complex. * 2002 (Heisei 14): Reconstruction of Suwa, Hachiman, and Inari Shrines. == Deities == The primary deity of Suwa Shrine is {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方神}}, the second son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神}}. He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命}} (Asama Shrine) * {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神}} (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}} and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from [[JR East]]’s {{ill|Tachikawa Station|ja|立川駅}}.{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine, Tachikawa}} [[Category:Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Suwa Shrines]] [[Category:Tachikawa, Tokyo]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[category:most recent 500]][[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市)]]

He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命}} (Asama Shrine) * {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神}} (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}} and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from [[JR East]]’s {{ill|Tachikawa Station|ja|立川駅}}.{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine, Tachikawa}} [[Category:Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Suwa Shrines]] [[Category:Tachikawa, Tokyo]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[category:most recent 500]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[Category:kk]] [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市)]]

Suwa Shrine Main Hall |location = 1-5-15 Shibasaki-cho, Tachikawa City, Tokyo{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=4}} |coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|43.4|N|139|24|22.0|E|region:JP-13_type:landmark|display=it}} |deity = {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方神}}{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}} |SacredObject = |Rank = {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=6}} |consecrated = July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2){{efn|The [[江戸名所図会]] gives the date as July 21{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}}.}} |MainHall = |Alias = Osuwasama{{Sfn|東京都神社庁|1986|p=322}} |Festival = Fourth Saturday and Sunday in August |Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|frame-height=150|frame-latitude=35.707|frame-longitude=139.405}}{{location map|Japan Tokyo|float=center|width=210}} }} '''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tachikawa City]], [[Tokyo]], Japan. Also referred to as '''Tachikawa Suwa Shrine''' (立川諏訪神社), it is renowned for its [[shishi-mai|lion dances]] and [[sumo]] performances.{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine 【Osuwasama】|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/tachikawa/3063/|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Association|accessdate=2019-05-13}} This article also covers the Hachiman Shrine, which is enshrined alongside Suwa Shrine’s main hall, and the subsidiary Inari Shrine. == History == ### Founding According to shrine records, Suwa Shrine was established on July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2), when the deity {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方命}} from {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} was enshrined in Shibasaki Village (modern-day Suwanomori Park, Tachikawa City).{{Cite book|title=Report on Tachikawa’s Shrine Architecture Survey|publisher=Tachikawa City Board of Education|year=1989|pages=106-107}}{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/history.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} The stone monument at Suwanomori Park bears the inscription, "June 27, Kōnin 2 – Enshrined Shinano Suwa Great deity." ### Fire of 1586 A wildfire occurred on November 9, 1586 (Tenshō 14), destroying the shrine's main hall, treasures, historical records, and the genealogy of the priests. ### Edo Period to Present Subsequent key events include: * 1670 (Kanbun 10): Relocation of the shrine and reconstruction of the main hall. * 1741 (Kanpō 1) & 1750 (Kan’en 3): Main hall rebuilt. * 1819 (Bunsei 2): Founding of the subsidiary Inari Shrine. * 1841 (Tenpō 12): Rebuilding of Hachiman Shrine after a fire in 1824 (Bunsei 7). * 1907 (Meiji 40): Hachiman Shrine deity incorporated into Suwa Shrine’s main hall. * 1994 (Heisei 6): Fire destroyed the shrine complex. * 2002 (Heisei 14): Reconstruction of Suwa, Hachiman, and Inari Shrines. == Deities == The primary deity of Suwa Shrine is {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方神}}, the second son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神}}. He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命}} (Asama Shrine) * {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神}} (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}} and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from [[JR East]]’s {{ill|Tachikawa Station|ja|立川駅}}.{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine, Tachikawa}} [[Category:Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Suwa Shrines]] [[Category:Tachikawa, Tokyo]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[Category:kk]] [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市)]]

Suwa Shrine Main Hall |location = 1-5-15 Shibasaki-cho, Tachikawa City, Tokyo{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=4}} |coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|43.4|N|139|24|22.0|E|region:JP-13_type:landmark|display=it}} |deity = {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方神}}{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}} |SacredObject = |Rank = {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}}{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=6}} |consecration_year = July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2){{efn|The [[江戸名所図会]] gives the date as July 21{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}}.}} |MainHall = |Alias = Osuwasama{{Sfn|東京都神社庁|1986|p=322}} |Festival = Fourth Saturday and Sunday in August |Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|frame-height=150|frame-latitude=35.707|frame-longitude=139.405}}{{location map|Japan Tokyo|float=center|width=210}} }} '''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tachikawa City]], [[Tokyo]], Japan. Also referred to as '''Tachikawa Suwa Shrine''' (立川諏訪神社), it is renowned for its [[shishi-mai|lion dances]] and [[sumo]] performances.{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine 【Osuwasama】|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/tachikawa/3063/|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Association|accessdate=2019-05-13}} This article also covers the Hachiman Shrine, which is enshrined alongside Suwa Shrine’s main hall, and the subsidiary Inari Shrine. == History == ### Founding According to shrine records, Suwa Shrine was established on July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2), when the deity {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方命}} from {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} was enshrined in Shibasaki Village (modern-day Suwanomori Park, Tachikawa City).{{Cite book|title=Report on Tachikawa’s Shrine Architecture Survey|publisher=Tachikawa City Board of Education|year=1989|pages=106-107}}{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/history.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} The stone monument at Suwanomori Park bears the inscription, "June 27, Kōnin 2 – Enshrined Shinano Suwa Great deity." ### Fire of 1586 A wildfire occurred on November 9, 1586 (Tenshō 14), destroying the shrine's main hall, treasures, historical records, and the genealogy of the priests. ### Edo Period to Present Subsequent key events include: * 1670 (Kanbun 10): Relocation of the shrine and reconstruction of the main hall. * 1741 (Kanpō 1) & 1750 (Kan’en 3): Main hall rebuilt. * 1819 (Bunsei 2): Founding of the subsidiary Inari Shrine. * 1841 (Tenpō 12): Rebuilding of Hachiman Shrine after a fire in 1824 (Bunsei 7). * 1907 (Meiji 40): Hachiman Shrine deity incorporated into Suwa Shrine’s main hall. * 1994 (Heisei 6): Fire destroyed the shrine complex. * 2002 (Heisei 14): Reconstruction of Suwa, Hachiman, and Inari Shrines. == Deities == The primary deity of Suwa Shrine is {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方神}}, the second son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神}}. He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命}} (Asama Shrine) * {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神}} (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}} and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from [[JR East]]’s {{ill|Tachikawa Station|ja|立川駅}}.{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine, Tachikawa}} [[Category:Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Suwa Shrines]] [[Category:Tachikawa, Tokyo]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[Category:kk]] [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市)]] [[Category:Oldest 50 pages as of February 16 2025]] [[Category:qq--qq]]

He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命}} (Asama Shrine) * {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神}} (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}} and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from [[JR East]]’s {{ill|Tachikawa Station|ja|立川駅}}.{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine, Tachikawa}} [[Category:Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Suwa Shrines]] [[Category:Tachikawa, Tokyo]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[Category:kk]] [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市)]] [[Category:Oldest 50 pages as of February 16 2025]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[Category:hashtag replacing]]

He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命}} (Asama Shrine) * {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神}} (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}} and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from [[JR East]]’s {{ill|Tachikawa Station|ja|立川駅}}.{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine, Tachikawa}} [[Category:Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Suwa Shrines]] [[Category:Tachikawa, Tokyo]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[Category:kk]] [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市)]] [[Category:Oldest 50 pages as of February 16 2025]] [[Category:hashtag replacing]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[Category:qq]]

He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命}} (Asama Shrine) * {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神}} (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}} and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from [[JR East]]’s {{ill|Tachikawa Station|ja|立川駅}}.{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine, Tachikawa}} [[Category:Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Suwa Shrines]] [[Category:Tachikawa, Tokyo]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[Category:kk]] [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市)]] [[Category:Oldest 50 pages as of February 16 2025]] [[Category:hashtag replacing]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[Category:qq]]

Also referred to as '''Tachikawa Suwa Shrine''' (立川諏訪神社), it is renowned for its [[shishi-mai|lion dances]] and [[sumo]] performances.{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine 【Osuwasama】|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/tachikawa/3063/|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Association|accessdate=2019-05-13}} This article also covers the Hachiman Shrine, which is enshrined alongside Suwa Shrine’s main hall, and the subsidiary Inari Shrine. == History == === Founding=== According to shrine records, Suwa Shrine was established on July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2), when the deity {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方命}} from {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} was enshrined in Shibasaki Village (modern-day Suwanomori Park, Tachikawa City).{{Cite book|title=Report on Tachikawa’s Shrine Architecture Survey|publisher=Tachikawa City Board of Education|year=1989|pages=106-107}}{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/history.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} The stone monument at Suwanomori Park bears the inscription, "June 27, Kōnin 2 – Enshrined Shinano Suwa Great deity." === Fire of 1586=== A wildfire occurred on November 9, 1586 (Tenshō 14), destroying the shrine's main hall, treasures, historical records, and the genealogy of the priests. === Edo Period to Present=== Subsequent key events include: * 1670 (Kanbun 10): Relocation of the shrine and reconstruction of the main hall. * 1741 (Kanpō 1) & 1750 (Kan’en 3): Main hall rebuilt. * 1819 (Bunsei 2): Founding of the subsidiary Inari Shrine. * 1841 (Tenpō 12): Rebuilding of Hachiman Shrine after a fire in 1824 (Bunsei 7). * 1907 (Meiji 40): Hachiman Shrine deity incorporated into Suwa Shrine’s main hall. * 1994 (Heisei 6): Fire destroyed the shrine complex. * 2002 (Heisei 14): Reconstruction of Suwa, Hachiman, and Inari Shrines. == Deities == The primary deity of Suwa Shrine is {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方神}}, the second son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神}}. He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命}} (Asama Shrine) * {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神}} (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}} and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from [[JR East]]’s {{ill|Tachikawa Station|ja|立川駅}}.{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine, Tachikawa}} [[Category:Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Suwa Shrines]] [[Category:Tachikawa, Tokyo]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[Category:kk]] [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市)]] [[Category:hashtag replacing]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[Category:qq]]

Also referred to as '''Tachikawa Suwa Shrine''' (立川諏訪神社), it is renowned for its [[shishi-mai|lion dances]] and [[sumo]] performances.{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine 【Osuwasama】|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/tachikawa/3063/|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Association|accessdate=2019-05-13}} This article also covers the Hachiman Shrine, which is enshrined alongside Suwa Shrine’s main hall, and the subsidiary Inari Shrine. == History == === Founding=== According to shrine records, Suwa Shrine was established on July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2), when the deity {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方命|12=draft}} from {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国|12=draft}} was enshrined in Shibasaki Village (modern-day Suwanomori Park, Tachikawa City).{{Cite book|title=Report on Tachikawa’s Shrine Architecture Survey|publisher=Tachikawa City Board of Education|year=1989|pages=106-107}}{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/history.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} The stone monument at Suwanomori Park bears the inscription, "June 27, Kōnin 2 – Enshrined Shinano Suwa Great deity." === Fire of 1586=== A wildfire occurred on November 9, 1586 (Tenshō 14), destroying the shrine's main hall, treasures, historical records, and the genealogy of the priests. === Edo Period to Present=== Subsequent key events include: * 1670 (Kanbun 10): Relocation of the shrine and reconstruction of the main hall. * 1741 (Kanpō 1) & 1750 (Kan’en 3): Main hall rebuilt. * 1819 (Bunsei 2): Founding of the subsidiary Inari Shrine. * 1841 (Tenpō 12): Rebuilding of Hachiman Shrine after a fire in 1824 (Bunsei 7). * 1907 (Meiji 40): Hachiman Shrine deity incorporated into Suwa Shrine’s main hall. * 1994 (Heisei 6): Fire destroyed the shrine complex. * 2002 (Heisei 14): Reconstruction of Suwa, Hachiman, and Inari Shrines. == Deities == The primary deity of Suwa Shrine is {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方神|12=draft}}, the second son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=draft}}. He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命|12=draft}} (Asama Shrine) * {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神|12=draft}} (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|12=draft}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=draft}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from [[JR East]]’s {{ill|Tachikawa Station|ja|立川駅|12=draft}}.{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Suwa Shrines]] [[Category:Tachikawa, Tokyo]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市)]] [[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suwa Shrine, Tachikawa}}

Suwa Shrine’s main hall. * 1994 (Heisei 6): Fire destroyed the shrine complex. * 2002 (Heisei 14): Reconstruction of Suwa, Hachiman, and Inari Shrines. == Deities == The primary deity of Suwa Shrine is {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方神|12=draft|12=draft}}, the second son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=draft|12=draft}}. He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命|12=draft|12=draft}} (Asama Shrine) * {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神|12=draft|12=draft}} (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|12=draft|12=draft}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=draft|12=draft}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from [[JR East]]’s {{ill|Tachikawa Station|ja|立川駅|12=draft|12=draft}}.{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Suwa Shrines]] [[Category:Tachikawa, Tokyo]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市)]] [[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]] {{citation needed|Suwa Shrine, Tachikawa}}

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Suwa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Suwa Shrine 20190513(2).jpg|250px|]]
Suwa Shrine Main Hall |location = 1-5-15 Shibasaki-cho, Tachikawa City, Tokyo{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=4}} |coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|43.4|N|139|24|22.0|E|region:JP-13_type:landmark|display=it}} |deity = {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方神|12=draft|12=draft}}{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}} |SacredObject = |Rank = {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|12=draft|12=draft}}{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=6}} |consecration_year = July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2){{efn|The [[江戸名所図会]] gives the date as July 21{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}}.}} |MainHall = |Alias = Osuwasama{{Sfn|東京都神社庁|1986|p=322}} |Festival = Fourth Saturday and Sunday in August |Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|frame-height=150|frame-latitude=35.707|frame-longitude=139.405}}{{location map|Japan Tokyo|float=center|width=210}} }} '''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tachikawa City]], [[Tokyo]], Japan. Also referred to as '''Tachikawa Suwa Shrine''' (立川諏訪神社), it is renowned for its [[shishi-mai|lion dances]] and [[sumo]] performances.{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine 【Osuwasama】|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/tachikawa/3063/|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Association|accessdate=2019-05-13}} This article also covers the Hachiman Shrine, which is enshrined alongside Suwa Shrine’s main hall, and the subsidiary Inari Shrine. == History == === Founding=== According to shrine records, Suwa Shrine was established on July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2), when the deity {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方命|12=draft|12=draft}} from {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国|12=draft|12=draft}} was enshrined in Shibasaki Village (modern-day Suwanomori Park, Tachikawa City).{{Cite book|title=Report on Tachikawa’s Shrine Architecture Survey|publisher=Tachikawa City Board of Education|year=1989|pages=106-107}}{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/history.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} The stone monument at Suwanomori Park bears the inscription, "June 27, Kōnin 2 – Enshrined Shinano Suwa Great deity." === Fire of 1586=== A wildfire occurred on November 9, 1586 (Tenshō 14), destroying the shrine's main hall, treasures, historical records, and the genealogy of the priests. === Edo Period to Present=== Subsequent key events include: * 1670 (Kanbun 10): Relocation of the shrine and reconstruction of the main hall. * 1741 (Kanpō 1) & 1750 (Kan’en 3): Main hall rebuilt. * 1819 (Bunsei 2): Founding of the subsidiary Inari Shrine. * 1841 (Tenpō 12): Rebuilding of Hachiman Shrine after a fire in 1824 (Bunsei 7). * 1907 (Meiji 40): Hachiman Shrine deity incorporated into Suwa Shrine’s main hall. * 1994 (Heisei 6): Fire destroyed the shrine complex. * 2002 (Heisei 14): Reconstruction of Suwa, Hachiman, and Inari Shrines. == Deities == The primary deity of Suwa Shrine is {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方神|12=draft|12=draft}}, the second son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=draft|12=draft}}. He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命|12=draft|12=draft}} (Asama Shrine) * {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神|12=draft|12=draft}} (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|12=draft|12=draft}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=draft|12=draft}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from [[JR East]]’s {{ill|Tachikawa Station|ja|立川駅|12=draft|12=draft}}.{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Suwa Shrines]] [[Category:Tachikawa, Tokyo]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市)]] [[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]] {{citation needed|Suwa Shrine, Tachikawa}}

Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from [[JR East]]’s {{ill|Tachikawa Station|ja|立川駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] {{citation needed|Suwa Shrine, Tachikawa}} {{translated page|ja|諏訪神社 (立川市)|version=72737446|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Suwa Shrines]] [[Category:Tachikawa, Tokyo]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]] [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市)]]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Suwa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Suwa Shrine 20190513(2).jpg|250px|]]
Suwa Shrine Main Hall |location = 1-5-15 Shibasaki-cho, Tachikawa City, Tokyo{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=4}} |coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|43.4|N|139|24|22.0|E|region:JP-13_type:landmark|display=it}} |deity = {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}} |SacredObject = |Rank = {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=6}} |consecration_year = July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2){{efn|The [[江戸名所図会]] gives the date as July 21{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}}.}} |MainHall = |Alias = Osuwasama{{Sfn|東京都神社庁|1986|p=322}} |Festival = Fourth Saturday and Sunday in August |Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|frame-height=150|frame-latitude=35.707|frame-longitude=139.405}}{{location map|Japan Tokyo|float=center|width=210}} }} '''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tachikawa City]], [[Tokyo]], Japan. Also referred to as '''Tachikawa Suwa Shrine''' (立川諏訪神社), it is renowned for its [[shishi-mai|lion dances]] and [[sumo]] performances.{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine 【Osuwasama】|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/tachikawa/3063/|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Association|accessdate=2019-05-13}} This article also covers the Hachiman Shrine, which is enshrined alongside Suwa Shrine’s main hall, and the subsidiary Inari Shrine. == History == === Founding=== According to shrine records, Suwa Shrine was established on July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2), when the deity {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} from {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was enshrined in Shibasaki Village (modern-day Suwanomori Park, Tachikawa City).{{Cite book|title=Report on Tachikawa’s Shrine Architecture Survey|publisher=Tachikawa City Board of Education|year=1989|pages=106-107}}{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/history.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} The stone monument at Suwanomori Park bears the inscription, "June 27, Kōnin 2 – Enshrined Shinano Suwa Great deity." === Fire of 1586=== A wildfire occurred on November 9, 1586 (Tenshō 14), destroying the shrine's main hall, treasures, historical records, and the genealogy of the priests. === Edo Period to Present=== Subsequent key events include: * 1670 (Kanbun 10): Relocation of the shrine and reconstruction of the main hall. * 1741 (Kanpō 1) & 1750 (Kan’en 3): Main hall rebuilt. * 1819 (Bunsei 2): Founding of the subsidiary Inari Shrine. * 1841 (Tenpō 12): Rebuilding of Hachiman Shrine after a fire in 1824 (Bunsei 7). * 1907 (Meiji 40): Hachiman Shrine deity incorporated into Suwa Shrine’s main hall. * 1994 (Heisei 6): Fire destroyed the shrine complex. * 2002 (Heisei 14): Reconstruction of Suwa, Hachiman, and Inari Shrines. == Deities == The primary deity of Suwa Shrine is {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the second son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Asama Shrine) * {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from [[JR East]]’s {{ill|Tachikawa Station|ja|立川駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] {{citation needed|Suwa Shrine, Tachikawa}} {{translated page|ja|諏訪神社 (立川市)|version=72737446|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[Category:Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Suwa Shrines]] [[Category:Tachikawa, Tokyo]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]] }} [[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]] [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市)]]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Suwa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Suwa Shrine 20190513(2).jpg|250px|]]

He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Asama Shrine) * {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from [[JR East]]’s {{ill|Tachikawa Station|ja|立川駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] [[Category:translated pages without meta categories]]\n{{translated page|ja|諏訪神社 (立川市)|version=72737446|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[Category:Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Suwa Shrines]] [[Category:Tachikawa, Tokyo]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]] }} [[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]] [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市)]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Suwa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Suwa Shrine 20190513(2).jpg|250px|]]
Suwa Shrine Main Hall |location = 1-5-15 Shibasaki-cho, Tachikawa City, Tokyo{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=4}} |coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|43.4|N|139|24|22.0|E|region:JP-13_type:landmark|display=it}} |deity = {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}} |SacredObject = |Rank = {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=6}} |consecration_year = July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2){{efn|The [[江戸名所図会]] gives the date as July 21{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}}.}} |MainHall = |Alias = Osuwasama{{Sfn|東京都神社庁|1986|p=322}} |Festival = Fourth Saturday and Sunday in August |Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|frame-height=150|frame-latitude=35.707|frame-longitude=139.405}}{{location map|Japan Tokyo|float=center|width=210}} }} '''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tachikawa City]], [[Tokyo]], Japan. Also referred to as '''Tachikawa Suwa Shrine''' (立川諏訪神社), it is renowned for its [[shishi-mai|lion dances]] and [[sumo]] performances.{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine 【Osuwasama】|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/tachikawa/3063/|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Association|accessdate=2019-05-13}} This article also covers the Hachiman Shrine, which is enshrined alongside Suwa Shrine’s main hall, and the subsidiary Inari Shrine. == History == === Founding=== According to shrine records, Suwa Shrine was established on July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2), when the deity {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} from {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was enshrined in Shibasaki Village (modern-day Suwanomori Park, Tachikawa City).{{Cite book|title=Report on Tachikawa’s Shrine Architecture Survey|publisher=Tachikawa City Board of Education|year=1989|pages=106-107}}{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/history.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} The stone monument at Suwanomori Park bears the inscription, "June 27, Kōnin 2 – Enshrined Shinano Suwa Great deity." === Fire of 1586=== A wildfire occurred on November 9, 1586 (Tenshō 14), destroying the shrine's main hall, treasures, historical records, and the genealogy of the priests. === Edo Period to Present=== Subsequent key events include: * 1670 (Kanbun 10): Relocation of the shrine and reconstruction of the main hall. * 1741 (Kanpō 1) & 1750 (Kan’en 3): Main hall rebuilt. * 1819 (Bunsei 2): Founding of the subsidiary Inari Shrine. * 1841 (Tenpō 12): Rebuilding of Hachiman Shrine after a fire in 1824 (Bunsei 7). * 1907 (Meiji 40): Hachiman Shrine deity incorporated into Suwa Shrine’s main hall. * 1994 (Heisei 6): Fire destroyed the shrine complex. * 2002 (Heisei 14): Reconstruction of Suwa, Hachiman, and Inari Shrines. == Deities == The primary deity of Suwa Shrine is {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the second son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Asama Shrine) * {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from [[JR East]]’s {{ill|Tachikawa Station|ja|立川駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] [[Category:translated pages without meta categories]] {{translated page|ja|諏訪神社 (立川市)|version=72737446|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[Category:Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Suwa Shrines]] [[Category:Tachikawa, Tokyo]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]] }} [[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]] [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市)]]

Kami|ja|大山咋神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamakui no Kami|qq=}} (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sutoku|qq=}} and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|qq=}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from [[JR East]]’s {{ill|Tachikawa {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Suwa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Suwa Shrine 20190513(2).jpg|250px|]]
Suwa Shrine Main Hall |location = 1-5-15 Shibasaki-cho, Tachikawa City, Tokyo{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=4}} |coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|43.4|N|139|24|22.0|E|region:JP-13_type:landmark|display=it}} |deity = {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeminakata no Mikoto|qq=}}{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}} |SacredObject = |Rank = {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=}}{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=6}} |consecration_year = July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2){{efn|The [[江戸名所図会]] gives the date as July 21{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}}.}} |MainHall = |Alias = Osuwasama{{Sfn|東京都神社庁|1986|p=322}} |Festival = Fourth Saturday and Sunday in August |Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|frame-height=150|frame-latitude=35.707|frame-longitude=139.405}}{{location map|Japan Tokyo|float=center|width=210}} }} '''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tachikawa City]], [[Tokyo]], Japan. Also referred to as '''Tachikawa Suwa Shrine''' (立川諏訪神社), it is renowned for its [[shishi-mai|lion dances]] and [[sumo]] performances.{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine 【Osuwasama】|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/tachikawa/3063/|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Association|accessdate=2019-05-13}} This article also covers the Hachiman Shrine, which is enshrined alongside Suwa Shrine’s main hall, and the subsidiary Inari Shrine. == History == === Founding=== According to shrine records, Suwa Shrine was established on July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2), when the deity {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeminakata no Mikoto|qq=}} from {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Province|qq=}} was enshrined in Shibasaki Village (modern-day Suwanomori Park, Tachikawa City).{{Cite book|title=Report on Tachikawa’s Shrine Architecture Survey|publisher=Tachikawa City Board of Education|year=1989|pages=106-107}}{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/history.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} The stone monument at Suwanomori Park bears the inscription, "June 27, Kōnin 2 – Enshrined Shinano Suwa Great deity." === Fire of 1586=== A wildfire occurred on November 9, 1586 (Tenshō 14), destroying the shrine's main hall, treasures, historical records, and the genealogy of the priests. === Edo Period to Present=== Subsequent key events include: * 1670 (Kanbun 10): Relocation of the shrine and reconstruction of the main hall. * 1741 (Kanpō 1) & 1750 (Kan’en 3): Main hall rebuilt. * 1819 (Bunsei 2): Founding of the subsidiary Inari Shrine. * 1841 (Tenpō 12): Rebuilding of Hachiman Shrine after a fire in 1824 (Bunsei 7). * 1907 (Meiji 40): Hachiman Shrine deity incorporated into Suwa Shrine’s main hall. * 1994 (Heisei 6): Fire destroyed the shrine complex. * 2002 (Heisei 14): Reconstruction of Suwa, Hachiman, and Inari Shrines. == Deities == The primary deity of Suwa Shrine is {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeminakata no Mikoto|qq=}}, the second son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}}. He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|qq=}} (Asama Shrine) * {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamakui no Kami|qq=}} (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor {{ill|Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sutoku|qq=}} and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|qq=}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from [[JR East]]’s {{ill|Tachikawa Station|ja|立川駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachikawa Station|qq=}}.{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|諏訪神社 (立川市)|version=72737446|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[Category:Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Suwa Shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] }} [[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]] [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市)]]

was enshrined in Shibasaki Village (modern-day Suwanomori Park, Tachikawa City).{{Cite book|title=Report on Tachikawa’s Shrine Architecture Survey|publisher=Tachikawa City Board of Education|year=1989|pages=106-107}}{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/history.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} The stone monument at Suwanomori Park bears the inscription, "June 27, Kōnin 2 – Enshrined Shinano Suwa Great deity." === Fire of 1586=== A wildfire occurred on November 9, 1586 (Tenshō 14), destroying the shrine's main hall, treasures, historical records, and the genealogy of the priests. === Edo Period to Present=== Subsequent key events include: * 1670 (Kanbun 10): Relocation of the shrine and reconstruction of the main hall. * 1741 (Kanpō 1) & 1750 (Kan’en 3): Main hall rebuilt. * 1819 (Bunsei 2): Founding of the subsidiary Inari Shrine. * 1841 (Tenpō 12): Rebuilding of Hachiman Shrine after a fire in 1824 (Bunsei 7). * 1907 (Meiji 40): Hachiman Shrine deity incorporated into Suwa Shrine’s main hall. * 1994 (Heisei 6): Fire destroyed the shrine complex. * 2002 (Heisei 14): Reconstruction of Suwa, Hachiman, and Inari Shrines. == Deities == The primary deity of Suwa Shrine is [[:en:Takeminakata|Takeminakata]], the second son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}. He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} (Asama Shrine) * [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]] (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor [[:en:Emperor Sutoku|Sutoku]] and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from [[JR East]]’s [[:en:Tachikawa Station|Tachikawa Station]].{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|諏訪神社 (立川市)|version=72737446|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[Category:Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Suwa Shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] }} [[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]] [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市)]]
