{{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} (Asama Shrine) * [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]] (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor [[:en:Emperor Sutoku|Sutoku]] and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]]’s [[:en:Tachikawa Station|Tachikawa Station]].{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[en:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]]}} [[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]] [[sn:諏訪神社 (立川市)]] [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市柴崎町)]] [[Category:Ja import oversized]] {{translated page|ja|諏訪神社 revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:29, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Suwa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Suwa Shrine 20190513(2).jpg|250px|]]
Suwa Shrine Main Hall |location = 1-5-15 Shibasaki-cho, Tachikawa City, Tokyo{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=4}} |coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|43.4|N|139|24|22.0|E|region:JP-13_type:landmark|display=it}} |deity = [[:en:Takeminakata|Takeminakata]]{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}} |SacredObject = |Rank = {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|qid=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=6}} |consecration_year = July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2){{efn|The [[江戸名所図会]] gives the date as July 21{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}}.}} |MainHall = |Alias = Osuwasama{{Sfn|東京都神社庁|1986|p=322}} |Festival = Fourth Saturday and Sunday in August |Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|frame-height=150|frame-latitude=35.707|frame-longitude=139.405}}{{location map|Japan Tokyo|float=center|width=210}} }} '''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tachikawa City]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], Japan. Also referred to as '''Tachikawa Suwa Shrine''' (立川諏訪神社), it is renowned for its [[shishi-mai|lion dances]] and [[:en:Sumo|Sumo]] performances.{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine 【Osuwasama】|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/tachikawa/3063/|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Association|accessdate=2019-05-13}} This article also covers the Hachiman Shrine, which is enshrined alongside Suwa Shrine’s main hall, and the subsidiary Inari Shrine. == History == === Founding=== According to shrine records, Suwa Shrine was established on July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2), when the deity {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeminakata no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タケミナカタ|1=Takeminakata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9150391}} from [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]] was enshrined in Shibasaki Village (modern-day Suwanomori Park, Tachikawa City).{{Cite book|title=Report on Tachikawa’s Shrine Architecture Survey|publisher=Tachikawa City Board of Education|year=1989|pages=106-107}}{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/history.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} The stone monument at Suwanomori Park bears the inscription, "June 27, Kōnin 2 – Enshrined Shinano Suwa Great deity." === Fire of 1586=== A wildfire occurred on November 9, 1586 (Tenshō 14), destroying the shrine's main hall, treasures, historical records, and the genealogy of the priests. === Edo Period to Present=== Subsequent key events include: * 1670 (Kanbun 10): Relocation of the shrine and reconstruction of the main hall. * 1741 (Kanpō 1) & 1750 (Kan’en 3): Main hall rebuilt. * 1819 (Bunsei 2): Founding of the subsidiary Inari Shrine. * 1841 (Tenpō 12): Rebuilding of Hachiman Shrine after a fire in 1824 (Bunsei 7). * 1907 (Meiji 40): Hachiman Shrine deity incorporated into Suwa Shrine’s main hall. * 1994 (Heisei 6): Fire destroyed the shrine complex. * 2002 (Heisei 14): Reconstruction of Suwa, Hachiman, and Inari Shrines. == Deities == The primary deity of Suwa Shrine is [[:en:Takeminakata|Takeminakata]], the second son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}. He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} (Asama Shrine) * [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]] (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor [[:en:Emperor Sutoku|Sutoku]] and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]]’s [[:en:Tachikawa Station|Tachikawa Station]].{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{draft categories| [[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]][[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[en:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]]}} [[sn:Suwa Shrine (Tachikawa)]] [[sn:諏訪神社 (立川市)]] [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市柴崎町)]] [[Category:Ja import oversized]] {{translated page|ja|諏訪神社 (立川市柴崎町)|version=105678421|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[Category:Missing wikidata]]

Suwa Shrine Main Hall |location = 1-5-15 Shibasaki-cho, Tachikawa City, Tokyo{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=4}} |coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|43.4|N|139|24|22.0|E|region:JP-13_type:landmark|display=it}} |deity = [[:en:Takeminakata|Takeminakata]]{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}} |SacredObject = |Rank = {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|qid=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=6}} |consecration_year = July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2){{efn|The [[江戸名所図会]] gives the date as July 21{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}}.}} |MainHall = |Alias = Osuwasama{{Sfn|東京都神社庁|1986|p=322}} |Festival = Fourth Saturday and Sunday in August |Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|frame-height=150|frame-latitude=35.707|frame-longitude=139.405}}{{location map|Japan Tokyo|float=center|width=210}} }} '''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tachikawa City]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], Japan. Also referred to as '''Tachikawa Suwa Shrine''' (立川諏訪神社), it is renowned for its [[shishi-mai|lion dances]] and [[:en:Sumo|Sumo]] performances.{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine 【Osuwasama】|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/tachikawa/3063/|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Association|accessdate=2019-05-13}} This article also covers the Hachiman Shrine, which is enshrined alongside Suwa Shrine’s main hall, and the subsidiary Inari Shrine. == History == === Founding=== According to shrine records, Suwa Shrine was established on July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2), when the deity {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeminakata no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タケミナカタ|1=Takeminakata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9150391}} from [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]] was enshrined in Shibasaki Village (modern-day Suwanomori Park, Tachikawa City).{{Cite book|title=Report on Tachikawa’s Shrine Architecture Survey|publisher=Tachikawa City Board of Education|year=1989|pages=106-107}}{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/history.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} The stone monument at Suwanomori Park bears the inscription, "June 27, Kōnin 2 – Enshrined Shinano Suwa Great deity." === Fire of 1586=== A wildfire occurred on November 9, 1586 (Tenshō 14), destroying the shrine's main hall, treasures, historical records, and the genealogy of the priests. === Edo Period to Present=== Subsequent key events include: * 1670 (Kanbun 10): Relocation of the shrine and reconstruction of the main hall. * 1741 (Kanpō 1) & 1750 (Kan’en 3): Main hall rebuilt. * 1819 (Bunsei 2): Founding of the subsidiary Inari Shrine. * 1841 (Tenpō 12): Rebuilding of Hachiman Shrine after a fire in 1824 (Bunsei 7). * 1907 (Meiji 40): Hachiman Shrine deity incorporated into Suwa Shrine’s main hall. * 1994 (Heisei 6): Fire destroyed the shrine complex. * 2002 (Heisei 14): Reconstruction of Suwa, Hachiman, and Inari Shrines. == Deities == The primary deity of Suwa Shrine is [[:en:Takeminakata|Takeminakata]], the second son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}. He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} (Asama Shrine) * [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]] (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor [[:en:Emperor Sutoku|Sutoku]] and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]]’s [[:en:Tachikawa Station|Tachikawa Station]].{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] }} [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市柴崎町)]] [[Category:Ja import oversized]] [[Category:Translated page but missing wikidata]]{{translated page|ja|諏訪神社 (立川市柴崎町)|version=105678421|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[Category:Missing wikidata]]

Suwa Shrine Main Hall |location = 1-5-15 Shibasaki-cho, Tachikawa City, Tokyo{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=4}} |coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|43.4|N|139|24|22.0|E|region:JP-13_type:landmark|display=it}} |deity = [[:en:Takeminakata|Takeminakata]]{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}} |SacredObject = |Rank = {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=6}} |consecration_year = July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2){{efn|The [[江戸名所図会]] gives the date as July 21{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}}.}} |MainHall = |Alias = Osuwasama{{Sfn|東京都神社庁|1986|p=322}} |Festival = Fourth Saturday and Sunday in August |Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|frame-height=150|frame-latitude=35.707|frame-longitude=139.405}}{{location map|Japan Tokyo|float=center|width=210}} }} '''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tachikawa City]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], Japan. Also referred to as '''Tachikawa Suwa Shrine''' (立川諏訪神社), it is renowned for its [[shishi-mai|lion dances]] and [[:en:Sumo|Sumo]] performances.{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine 【Osuwasama】|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/tachikawa/3063/|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Association|accessdate=2019-05-13}} This article also covers the Hachiman Shrine, which is enshrined alongside Suwa Shrine’s main hall, and the subsidiary Inari Shrine. == History == === Founding=== According to shrine records, Suwa Shrine was established on July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2), when the deity {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タケミナカタ|1=Takeminakata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9150391}} from [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]] was enshrined in Shibasaki Village (modern-day Suwanomori Park, Tachikawa City).{{Cite book|title=Report on Tachikawa’s Shrine Architecture Survey|publisher=Tachikawa City Board of Education|year=1989|pages=106-107}}{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/history.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} The stone monument at Suwanomori Park bears the inscription, "June 27, Kōnin 2 – Enshrined Shinano Suwa Great deity." === Fire of 1586=== A wildfire occurred on November 9, 1586 (Tenshō 14), destroying the shrine's main hall, treasures, historical records, and the genealogy of the priests. === Edo Period to Present=== Subsequent key events include: * 1670 (Kanbun 10): Relocation of the shrine and reconstruction of the main hall. * 1741 (Kanpō 1) & 1750 (Kan’en 3): Main hall rebuilt. * 1819 (Bunsei 2): Founding of the subsidiary Inari Shrine. * 1841 (Tenpō 12): Rebuilding of Hachiman Shrine after a fire in 1824 (Bunsei 7). * 1907 (Meiji 40): Hachiman Shrine deity incorporated into Suwa Shrine’s main hall. * 1994 (Heisei 6): Fire destroyed the shrine complex. * 2002 (Heisei 14): Reconstruction of Suwa, Hachiman, and Inari Shrines. == Deities == The primary deity of Suwa Shrine is [[:en:Takeminakata|Takeminakata]], the second son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}. He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} (Asama Shrine) * [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]] (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor [[:en:Emperor Sutoku|Sutoku]] and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]]’s [[:en:Tachikawa Station|Tachikawa Station]].{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] }} [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市柴崎町)]] [[Category:Ja import oversized]] {{translated page|ja|諏訪神社 (立川市柴崎町)|version=105678421|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{wikidata link|Q63788026}}

Suwa Shrine Main Hall |location = 1-5-15 Shibasaki-cho, Tachikawa City, Tokyo{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=4}} |coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|43.4|N|139|24|22.0|E|region:JP-13_type:landmark|display=it}} |deity = [[:en:Takeminakata|Takeminakata]]{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}} |SacredObject = |Rank = {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=6}} |consecration_year = July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2){{efn|The [[江戸名所図会]] gives the date as July 21{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}}.}} |MainHall = |Alias = Osuwasama{{Sfn|東京都神社庁|1986|p=322}} |Festival = Fourth Saturday and Sunday in August |Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|frame-height=150|frame-latitude=35.707|frame-longitude=139.405}}{{location map|Japan Tokyo|float=center|width=210}} }} '''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tachikawa City]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], Japan. Also referred to as '''Tachikawa Suwa Shrine''' (立川諏訪神社), it is renowned for its [[shishi-mai|lion dances]] and [[:en:Sumo|Sumo]] performances.{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine 【Osuwasama】|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/tachikawa/3063/|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Association|accessdate=2019-05-13}} This article also covers the Hachiman Shrine, which is enshrined alongside Suwa Shrine’s main hall, and the subsidiary Inari Shrine. == History == === Founding=== According to shrine records, Suwa Shrine was established on July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2), when the deity {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タケミナカタ|1=Takeminakata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9150391}} from [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]] was enshrined in Shibasaki Village (modern-day Suwanomori Park, Tachikawa City).{{Cite book|title=Report on Tachikawa’s Shrine Architecture Survey|publisher=Tachikawa City Board of Education|year=1989|pages=106-107}}{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/history.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} The stone monument at Suwanomori Park bears the inscription, "June 27, Kōnin 2 – Enshrined Shinano Suwa Great deity." === Fire of 1586=== A wildfire occurred on November 9, 1586 (Tenshō 14), destroying the shrine's main hall, treasures, historical records, and the genealogy of the priests. === Edo Period to Present=== Subsequent key events include: * 1670 (Kanbun 10): Relocation of the shrine and reconstruction of the main hall. * 1741 (Kanpō 1) & 1750 (Kan’en 3): Main hall rebuilt. * 1819 (Bunsei 2): Founding of the subsidiary Inari Shrine. * 1841 (Tenpō 12): Rebuilding of Hachiman Shrine after a fire in 1824 (Bunsei 7). * 1907 (Meiji 40): Hachiman Shrine deity incorporated into Suwa Shrine’s main hall. * 1994 (Heisei 6): Fire destroyed the shrine complex. * 2002 (Heisei 14): Reconstruction of Suwa, Hachiman, and Inari Shrines. == Deities == The primary deity of Suwa Shrine is [[:en:Takeminakata|Takeminakata]], the second son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}. He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} (Asama Shrine) * [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]] (Hiyoshi redirects to 大物主}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite Suwa Shrine Main Hall |location = 1-5-15 Shibasaki-cho, Tachikawa City, Tokyo{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=4}} |coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|43.4|N|139|24|22.0|E|region:JP-13_type:landmark|display=it}} |deity = [[:en:Takeminakata|Takeminakata]]{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}} |SacredObject = |Rank = {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=6}} |consecration_year = July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2){{efn|The [[江戸名所図会]] gives the date as July 21{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}}.}} |MainHall = |Alias = Osuwasama{{Sfn|東京都神社庁|1986|p=322}} |Festival = Fourth Saturday and Sunday in August |Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|frame-height=150|frame-latitude=35.707|frame-longitude=139.405}}{{location map|Japan Tokyo|float=center|width=210}} }} '''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tachikawa City]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], Japan. Also referred to as '''Tachikawa Suwa Shrine''' (立川諏訪神社), it is renowned for its [[shishi-mai|lion dances]] and [[:en:Sumo|Sumo]] performances.{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine 【Osuwasama】|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/tachikawa/3063/|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Association|accessdate=2019-05-13}} This article also covers the Hachiman Shrine, which is enshrined alongside Suwa Shrine’s main hall, and the subsidiary Inari Shrine. == History == === Founding=== According to shrine records, Suwa Shrine was established on July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2), when the deity {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タケミナカタ|1=Takeminakata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9150391}} from [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]] was enshrined in Shibasaki Village (modern-day Suwanomori Park, Tachikawa City).{{Cite book|title=Report on Tachikawa’s Shrine Architecture Survey|publisher=Tachikawa City Board of Education|year=1989|pages=106-107}}{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/history.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} The stone monument at Suwanomori Park bears the inscription, "June 27, Kōnin 2 – Enshrined Shinano Suwa Great deity." === Fire of 1586=== A wildfire occurred on November 9, 1586 (Tenshō 14), destroying the shrine's main hall, treasures, historical records, and the genealogy of the priests. === Edo Period to Present=== Subsequent key events include: * 1670 (Kanbun 10): Relocation of the shrine and reconstruction of the main hall. * 1741 (Kanpō 1) & 1750 (Kan’en 3): Main hall rebuilt. * 1819 (Bunsei 2): Founding of the subsidiary Inari Shrine. * 1841 (Tenpō 12): Rebuilding of Hachiman Shrine after a fire in 1824 (Bunsei 7). * 1907 (Meiji 40): Hachiman Shrine deity incorporated into Suwa Shrine’s main hall. * 1994 (Heisei 6): Fire destroyed the shrine complex. * 2002 (Heisei 14): Reconstruction of Suwa, Hachiman, and Inari Shrines. == Deities == The primary deity of Suwa Shrine is [[:en:Takeminakata|Takeminakata]], the second son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}. He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} (Asama Shrine) * [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]] (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor [[:en:Emperor Sutoku|Sutoku]] and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]]’s [[:en:Tachikawa Station|Tachikawa Station]].{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] }} [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市柴崎町)]] [[Category:Ja import oversized]] {{translated page|ja|諏訪神社 (立川市柴崎町)|version=105678421|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{wikidata link|Q63788026}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

{{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タケミナカタ|1=Takeminakata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9150391}} from [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]] was enshrined in Shibasaki Village (modern-day Suwanomori Park, Tachikawa City).{{Cite book|title=Report on Tachikawa’s Shrine Architecture Survey|publisher=Tachikawa City Board of Education|year=1989|pages=106-107}}{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/history.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} The stone after a fire in 1824 (Bunsei 7). * 1907 (Meiji 40): Hachiman Shrine deity incorporated into Suwa Shrine’s main hall. * 1994 (Heisei 6): Fire destroyed the shrine complex. * 2002 (Heisei 14): Reconstruction of Suwa, Hachiman, and Inari Shrines. == Deities == The primary deity of Suwa Shrine is [[:en:Takeminakata|Takeminakata]], the second son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}. He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} (Asama Shrine) * [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]] (Hiyoshi redirects to 大物主}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite reconstruction of the main hall. * 1741 (Kanpō 1) & 1750 (Kan’en 3): Main hall rebuilt. * 1819 (Bunsei 2): Founding of the subsidiary Inari Shrine. * 1841 (Tenpō 12): Rebuilding of Hachiman Shrine after a fire in 1824 (Bunsei 7). * 1907 (Meiji 40): Hachiman Shrine deity incorporated into Suwa Shrine’s main hall. * 1994 (Heisei 6): Fire destroyed the shrine complex. * 2002 (Heisei 14): Reconstruction of Suwa, Hachiman, and Inari Shrines. == Deities == The primary deity of Suwa Shrine is [[:en:Takeminakata|Takeminakata]], the second son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}. He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} (Asama Shrine) * [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]] (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor [[:en:Emperor Sutoku|Sutoku]] and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]]’s [[:en:Tachikawa Station|Tachikawa Station]].{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Also referred to as '''Tachikawa Suwa Shrine''' (立川諏訪神社), it is renowned for its [[shishi-mai|lion dances]] and [[:en:Sumo|Sumo]] performances.{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine alongside Suwa Shrine’s main hall, and the subsidiary Inari Shrine. == History == === Founding=== According to shrine records, Suwa Shrine was established on July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2), when the deity {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タケミナカタ|1=Takeminakata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9150391}} from [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]] was enshrined in Shibasaki Village (modern-day Suwanomori Park, Tachikawa City).{{Cite book|title=Report on Tachikawa’s Shrine Architecture Survey|publisher=Tachikawa City Board of Education|year=1989|pages=106-107}}{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/history.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} The stone monument at Suwanomori Park bears the inscription, "June 27, Kōnin 2 – Enshrined Shinano Suwa Great deity." === Fire of 1586=== A wildfire occurred on November 9, 1586 (Tenshō 14), destroying the shrine's main hall, treasures, historical records, and the genealogy of the priests. === Edo Period to Present=== Subsequent key events include: * 1670 (Kanbun 10): Relocation of the shrine and reconstruction of the main hall. * 1741 (Kanpō 1) & 1750 (Kan’en 3): Main hall rebuilt. * 1819 (Bunsei 2): Founding of the subsidiary Inari Shrine. * 1841 (Tenpō 12): Rebuilding of Hachiman Shrine after a fire in 1824 (Bunsei 7). * 1907 (Meiji 40): Hachiman Shrine deity incorporated into Suwa Shrine’s main hall. * 1994 (Heisei 6): Fire destroyed the shrine complex. * 2002 (Heisei 14): Reconstruction of Suwa, Hachiman, and Inari Shrines. == Deities == The primary deity of Suwa Shrine is [[:en:Takeminakata|Takeminakata]], the second son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}. He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} (Asama Shrine) * [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]] (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor [[:en:Emperor Sutoku|Sutoku]] and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]]’s [[:en:Tachikawa Station|Tachikawa Station]].{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External [[commons:Category:Suwa Shrine, Tachikawa]][[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市柴崎町)]] [[commons:Category:Suwa Shrine, Tachikawa]][[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市柴崎町)]] [[sn:commons:Category:Suwa Shrine, Tachikawa]][[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市柴崎町)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:29, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Suwa Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Suwa Shrine 20190513(2).jpg|250px|]]
Suwa Shrine Main Hall |location = 1-5-15 Shibasaki-cho, Tachikawa City, Tokyo{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=4}} |coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|43.4|N|139|24|22.0|E|region:JP-13_type:landmark|display=it}} |deity = [[:en:Takeminakata|Takeminakata]]{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}} |SacredObject = |Rank = {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=6}} |consecration_year = July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2){{efn|The [[江戸名所図会]] gives the date as July 21{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}}.}} |MainHall = |Alias = Osuwasama{{Sfn|東京都神社庁|1986|p=322}} |Festival = Fourth Saturday and Sunday in August |Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|frame-height=150|frame-latitude=35.707|frame-longitude=139.405}}{{location map|Japan Tokyo|float=center|width=210}} }} '''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tachikawa City]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], Japan. Also referred to as '''Tachikawa Suwa Shrine''' (立川諏訪神社), it is renowned for its [[shishi-mai|lion dances]] and [[:en:Sumo|Sumo]] performances.{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine 【Osuwasama】|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/tachikawa/3063/|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Association|accessdate=2019-05-13}} This article also covers the Hachiman Shrine, which is enshrined alongside Suwa Shrine’s main hall, and the subsidiary Inari Shrine. == History == === Founding=== According to shrine records, Suwa Shrine was established on July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2), when the deity {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タケミナカタ|1=Takeminakata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9150391}} from [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]] was enshrined in Shibasaki Village (modern-day Suwanomori Park, Tachikawa City).{{Cite book|title=Report on Tachikawa’s Shrine Architecture Survey|publisher=Tachikawa City Board of Education|year=1989|pages=106-107}}{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/history.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} The stone monument at Suwanomori Park bears the inscription, "June 27, Kōnin 2 – Enshrined Shinano Suwa Great deity." === Fire of 1586=== A wildfire occurred on November 9, 1586 (Tenshō 14), destroying the shrine's main hall, treasures, historical records, and the genealogy of the priests. === Edo Period to Present=== Subsequent key events include: * 1670 (Kanbun 10): Relocation of the shrine and reconstruction of the main hall. * 1741 (Kanpō 1) & 1750 (Kan’en 3): Main hall rebuilt. * 1819 (Bunsei 2): Founding of the subsidiary Inari Shrine. * 1841 (Tenpō 12): Rebuilding of Hachiman Shrine after a fire in 1824 (Bunsei 7). * 1907 (Meiji 40): Hachiman Shrine deity incorporated into Suwa Shrine’s main hall. * 1994 (Heisei 6): Fire destroyed the shrine complex. * 2002 (Heisei 14): Reconstruction of Suwa, Hachiman, and Inari Shrines. == Deities == The primary deity of Suwa Shrine is [[:en:Takeminakata|Takeminakata]], the second son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}. He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} (Asama Shrine) * [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]] (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor [[:en:Emperor Sutoku|Sutoku]] and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]]’s [[:en:Tachikawa Station|Tachikawa Station]].{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市柴崎町)]] {{translated page|ja|諏訪神社 (立川市柴崎町)|version=105678421|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{wikidata link|Q63788026}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Ja import oversized]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

{{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タケミナカタ|1=Takeminakata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9150391}} from {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province}} was enshrined in Shibasaki Village (modern-day Suwanomori Park, Tachikawa City).{{Cite book|title=Report on Tachikawa’s Shrine Architecture Survey|publisher=Tachikawa City Board of Education|year=1989|pages=106-107}}{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/history.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} The stone monument at Suwanomori Park bears the inscription, "June 27, Kōnin 2 – Enshrined Shinano Suwa Great deity." === Fire of 1586=== A wildfire occurred on November 9, 1586 (Tenshō 14), destroying the shrine's main hall, treasures, historical records, and the genealogy of the priests. === Edo Period to Present=== Subsequent key events include: * 1670 (Kanbun 10): Relocation of the shrine and reconstruction of the main hall. * 1741 (Kanpō 1) & 1750 (Kan’en 3): Main hall rebuilt. * 1819 (Bunsei 2): Founding of the subsidiary Inari Shrine. * 1841 (Tenpō 12): Rebuilding of Hachiman Shrine after a fire in 1824 (Bunsei 7). * 1907 (Meiji 40): Hachiman Shrine deity incorporated into Suwa Shrine’s main hall. * 1994 (Heisei 6): Fire destroyed the shrine complex. * 2002 (Heisei 14): Reconstruction of Suwa, Hachiman, and Inari Shrines. == Deities == The primary deity of Suwa Shrine is {{ill|Takeminakata|en|Takeminakata|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata}}, the second son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}. He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} (Asama Shrine) * {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|en|Oyamakui no Kami|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ōyamakui no Kami}} (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|en|Emperor Sutoku|WD=Q349287|lt=Sutoku}} and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company}}’s {{ill|Tachikawa Station|en|Tachikawa Station|WD=Q801501|lt=Tachikawa Station}}.{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市柴崎町)]] {{translated page|ja|諏訪神社 (立川市柴崎町)|version=105678421|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{wikidata link|Q63788026}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Ja import oversized]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Suwa Shrine Main Hall |location = 1-5-15 Shibasaki-cho, Tachikawa City, Tokyo{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=4}} |coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|43.4|N|139|24|22.0|E|region:JP-13_type:landmark|display=it}} |deity = {{ill|Takeminakata|en|Takeminakata|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata}}{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}} |SacredObject = |Rank = {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=6}} |consecration_year = July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2){{efn|The [[江戸名所図会]] gives the date as July 21{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}}.}} |MainHall = |Alias = Osuwasama{{Sfn|東京都神社庁|1986|p=322}} |Festival = Fourth Saturday and Sunday in August |Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|frame-height=150|frame-latitude=35.707|frame-longitude=139.405}}{{location map|Japan Tokyo|float=center|width=210}} }} '''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tachikawa City]], {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}, Japan. Also referred to as '''Tachikawa Suwa Shrine''' (立川諏訪神社), it is renowned for its [[shishi-mai|lion dances]] and {{ill|Sumo|en|Sumo|WD=Q40561|lt=Sumo}} performances.{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine 【Osuwasama】|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/tachikawa/3063/|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Association|accessdate=2019-05-13}} This article also covers the Hachiman Shrine, which is enshrined alongside Suwa Shrine’s main hall, and the subsidiary Inari Shrine. == History == === Founding=== According to shrine records, Suwa Shrine was established on July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2), when the deity {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タケミナカタ|1=Takeminakata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9150391}} from {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province}} was enshrined in Shibasaki Village (modern-day Suwanomori Park, Tachikawa City).{{Cite book|title=Report on Tachikawa’s Shrine Architecture Survey|publisher=Tachikawa City Board of Education|year=1989|pages=106-107}}{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/history.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} The stone monument at Suwanomori Park bears the inscription, "June 27, Kōnin 2 – Enshrined Shinano Suwa Great deity." === Fire of 1586=== A wildfire occurred on November 9, 1586 (Tenshō 14), destroying the shrine's main hall, treasures, historical records, and the genealogy of the priests. === Edo Period to Present=== Subsequent key events include: * 1670 (Kanbun 10): Relocation of the shrine and reconstruction of the main hall. * 1741 (Kanpō 1) & 1750 (Kan’en 3): Main hall rebuilt. * 1819 (Bunsei 2): Founding of the subsidiary Inari Shrine. * 1841 (Tenpō 12): Rebuilding of Hachiman Shrine after a fire in 1824 (Bunsei 7). * 1907 (Meiji 40): Hachiman Shrine deity incorporated into Suwa Shrine’s main hall. * 1994 (Heisei 6): Fire destroyed the shrine complex. * 2002 (Heisei 14): Reconstruction of Suwa, Hachiman, and Inari Shrines. == Deities == The primary deity of Suwa Shrine is {{ill|Takeminakata|en|Takeminakata|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata}}, the second son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}. He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} (Asama Shrine) * {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|en|Oyamakui no Kami|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ōyamakui no Kami}} (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|en|Emperor Sutoku|WD=Q349287|lt=Sutoku}} and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company}}’s {{ill|Tachikawa Station|en|Tachikawa Station|WD=Q801501|lt=Tachikawa Station}}.{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市柴崎町)]] {{translated page|ja|諏訪神社 (立川市柴崎町)|version=105678421|comment=Imported full ja Suwa Shrine Main Hall |location = 1-5-15 Shibasaki-cho, Tachikawa City, Tokyo{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=4}} |coordinates = {{Coord|35|41|43.4|N|139|24|22.0|E|region:JP-13_type:landmark|display=it}} |deity = {{ill|Takeminakata|en|Takeminakata|WD=Q9150391|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata}}{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}} |SacredObject = |Rank = {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=6}} |consecration_year = July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2){{efn|The [[江戸名所図会]] gives the date as July 21{{Sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=394}}.}} |MainHall = |Alias = Osuwasama{{Sfn|東京都神社庁|1986|p=322}} |Festival = Fourth Saturday and Sunday in August |Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|frame-height=150|frame-latitude=35.707|frame-longitude=139.405}}{{location map|Japan Tokyo|float=center|width=210}} }} '''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Tachikawa City]], {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}, Japan. Also referred to as '''Tachikawa Suwa Shrine''' (立川諏訪神社), it is renowned for its [[shishi-mai|lion dances]] and {{ill|Sumo|en|Sumo|WD=Q40561|lt=Sumo}} performances.{{Cite web|title=Suwa Shrine 【Osuwasama】|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/tachikawa/3063/|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Association|accessdate=2019-05-13}} This article also covers the Hachiman Shrine, which is enshrined alongside Suwa Shrine’s main hall, and the subsidiary Inari Shrine. == History == === Founding=== According to shrine records, Suwa Shrine was established on July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2), when the deity {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タケミナカタ|1=Takeminakata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9150391}} from {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province}} was enshrined in Shibasaki Village (modern-day Suwanomori Park, Tachikawa City).{{Cite book|title=Report on Tachikawa’s Shrine Architecture Survey|publisher=Tachikawa City Board of Education|year=1989|pages=106-107}}{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/history.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} The stone monument at Suwanomori Park bears the inscription, "June 27, Kōnin 2 – Enshrined Shinano Suwa Great deity." === Fire of 1586=== A wildfire occurred on November 9, 1586 (Tenshō 14), destroying the shrine's main hall, treasures, historical records, and the genealogy of the priests. === Edo Period to Present=== Subsequent key events include: * 1670 (Kanbun 10): Relocation of the shrine and reconstruction of the main hall. * 1741 (Kanpō 1) & 1750 (Kan’en 3): Main hall rebuilt. * 1819 (Bunsei 2): Founding of the subsidiary Inari Shrine. * 1841 (Tenpō 12): Rebuilding of Hachiman Shrine after a fire in 1824 (Bunsei 7). * 1907 (Meiji 40): Hachiman Shrine deity incorporated into Suwa Shrine’s main hall. * 1994 (Heisei 6): Fire destroyed the shrine complex. * 2002 (Heisei 14): Reconstruction of Suwa, Hachiman, and Inari Shrines. == Deities == The primary deity of Suwa Shrine is {{ill|Takeminakata|en|Takeminakata|WD=Q9150391|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata}}, the second son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}. He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} (Asama Shrine) * {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|en|Oyamakui no Kami|WD=Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ōyamakui no Kami}} (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|en|Emperor Sutoku|WD=Q349287|lt=Sutoku}} and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company}}’s {{ill|Tachikawa Station|en|Tachikawa Station|WD=Q801501|lt=Tachikawa Station}}.{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市柴崎町)]] {{translated page|ja|諏訪神社 (立川市柴崎町)|version=105678421|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{wikidata link|Q63788026}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Ja import oversized]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

alongside Suwa Shrine’s main hall, and the subsidiary Inari Shrine. == History == === Founding=== According to shrine records, Suwa Shrine was established on July 27, 811 (Kōnin 2), when the deity {{ill|Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja|建御名方命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeminakata no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タケミナカタ|1=Takeminakata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9150391}} from {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province|1=Shinano Province}} was enshrined in Shibasaki Village (modern-day Suwanomori Park, Tachikawa City).{{Cite book|title=Report on Tachikawa’s Shrine Architecture Survey|publisher=Tachikawa City Board of Education|year=1989|pages=106-107}}{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/history.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} The stone monument at Suwanomori Park bears the inscription, "June 27, Kōnin 2 – Enshrined Shinano Suwa Great deity." === Fire of 1586=== A wildfire occurred on November 9, 1586 (Tenshō 14), destroying the shrine's main hall, treasures, historical records, and the genealogy of the priests. === Edo Period to Present=== Subsequent key events include: * 1670 (Kanbun 10): Relocation of the shrine and reconstruction of the main hall. * 1741 (Kanpō 1) & 1750 (Kan’en 3): Main hall rebuilt. * 1819 (Bunsei 2): Founding of the subsidiary Inari Shrine. * 1841 (Tenpō 12): Rebuilding of Hachiman Shrine after a fire in 1824 (Bunsei 7). * 1907 (Meiji 40): Hachiman Shrine deity incorporated into Suwa Shrine’s main hall. * 1994 (Heisei 6): Fire destroyed the shrine complex. * 2002 (Heisei 14): Reconstruction of Suwa, Hachiman, and Inari Shrines. == Deities == The primary deity of Suwa Shrine is {{ill|Takeminakata|en|Takeminakata|WD=Q9150391|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata|1=Takeminakata}}, the second son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}. He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} (Asama Shrine) * {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|en|Oyamakui no Kami|WD=Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ōyamakui no Kami|1=Oyamakui no Kami}} (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|en|Emperor Sutoku|WD=Q349287|lt=Sutoku|1=Emperor Sutoku}} and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company|1=East Japan Railway Company}}’s {{ill|Tachikawa Station|en|Tachikawa Station|WD=Q801501|lt=Tachikawa Station|1=Tachikawa Station}}.{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市柴崎町)]] {{translated page|ja|諏訪神社 (立川市柴崎町)|version=105678421|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{wikidata link|Q63788026}} [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Ja import oversized]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

redirects to 大国主}}. He is venerated as a god of war and agriculture.{{Sfn|宗教法人諏訪神社|1987|p=7}} The shrine also enshrines: * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花之佐久夜毘売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} (Asama Shrine) * {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|en|Oyamakui no Kami|WD=Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ōyamakui no Kami|1=Oyamakui no Kami}} (Hiyoshi Shrine) * Emperor {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|en|Emperor Sutoku|WD=Q349287|lt=Sutoku|1=Emperor Sutoku}} and {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大物主命|1=Ōmononushi|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} (Konpira Shrine) * {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (Hōsō Shrine) == Annual Events == The shrine hosts several annual events:{{Cite web|title=Annual Events|url=http://suwajinja.or.jp/event.html|publisher=Suwa Shrine|accessdate=2019-05-13}} * January 1: New Year Festival * February 3: Setsubun Festival * Fourth Saturday & Sunday in August: Grand Festival featuring sumo, lion dances, and mikoshi parades. * November 15: Shichi-Go-San (Seven-Five-Three) Festival == Cultural Significance == Suwa Shrine’s lion dances, known for their intricate performances, are a designated intangible folk cultural property of Tachikawa City.{{Cite journal|title=Preserving Traditional Arts: Lion Dances of Suwa Shrine|journal=Tama no Ayumi|publisher=Tama Culture Foundation}} == Access == The shrine is a 10-minute walk from {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company|1=East Japan Railway Company}}’s {{ill|Tachikawa Station|en|Tachikawa Station|WD=Q801501|lt=Tachikawa Station|1=Tachikawa Station}}.{{Cite book |title=Tokyo’s Hometown Stories|publisher=Sports Festival Tokyo Committee|year=2013}} == External Links == * [http://suwajinja.or.jp Official Website of Suwa Shrine] [[ja:諏訪神社 (立川市柴崎町)]] {{translated page|ja|諏訪神社
